caveats: a: No direct task-exposure study exists for NAICS 325520 or outsourced adhesive manufacturers.
a: Exposure varies by chemistry, batch size, quality certification, and existing automation.
rho: Case studies are selected and do not establish a representative implementation rate.
rho: This estimate excludes pricing and demand effects.
e: The provided firm count is an EBITDA-margin bridge, not an observed service-firm census.
e: Hybrid firms may have eligible toll operations alongside ineligible branded-product revenue.
s5: The cited ASC acquisition is a distributor, demonstrating buyer appetite but not a manufacturing transfer rate.
s5: No owner-age distribution specific to eligible adhesive firms was found.
q: Contract structures differ between customer-owned formula tolling and manufacturer-owned private label.
q: The estimate excludes implementation and volume risk.
d5: ASC combines adhesives and sealants across North America rather than US toll adhesives alone.
d5: The constant-quality service basket can shift as customers reformulate for VOC, PFAS, or sustainability requirements.
o: Some formulation screening, documentation, ordering, and technical support can shift to customer self-service.
o: Operator requirement does not imply unchanged staffing levels.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Brands and industrial users buy recurring batches, overflow capacity, custom formulations, private-label products, and associated QC under specifications and purchase orders; customers may own formulas in toll arrangements or rely on the manufacturer for formulation and compliance.
lens.deviation_from_default: Adhesive manufacturing contains proprietary product businesses and a distinct outsourced-production subset. The lens is narrowed to toll, contract, and private-label manufacturers because only that subset consistently supplies a recurring outsourced service to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers whose primary business is selling proprietary branded adhesives, captive plants, distributors without manufacturing, tape converters outside this NAICS, application contractors, and one-time laboratory projects without repeat outsourced production.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market toll, contract, and private-label adhesive manufacturers providing repeat formulation, scale-up, blending, testing, filling, packaging, and outsourced capacity to external brands and industrial customers.

a.bridge: NIST documents small-manufacturer AI use cases in predictive maintenance, quality, scrap, throughput, and forecasting. Adhesive-industry sources show formulation, R&D, QC, scale-up, blending, packaging, and customer-specific technical work. I translate these broader capabilities into wage-weighted exposure for LMM contract adhesive plants: formula and prior-batch retrieval, experiment design support, document review, scheduling, quoting, forecasting, and quality analytics are exposed, while charging, mixing, sampling, filling, cleaning, maintenance, and physical testing are not. Selected NIST successes likely overstate readiness in plants with fragmented data.
caveats: No direct task-exposure study exists for NAICS 325520 or outsourced adhesive manufacturers.
Exposure varies by chemistry, batch size, quality certification, and existing automation.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted activity census across chemists, QC, operators, planners, maintenance, supply chain, sales, and administration at five or more LMM toll adhesive plants would materially move the estimate.
a.rationale: Information-intensive technical and planning roles carry meaningful wages, but the service remains rooted in physical chemical processing and laboratory validation. Exposure is therefore material but below half of the wage-weighted task base.

d5.bridge: ASC forecasts combined North American adhesive and sealant volume to grow at 3.0% annually from 5.52 million tons in 2025 to 6.39 million tons in 2030, with the US representing 80.6% of regional volume. I apply approximately the forecast volume path to US outsourced adhesive manufacturing, widening downward for in-house production and end-market mix and upward for private-label and capacity outsourcing. Because sealants and all business models are included, the source may overstate or understate the service subset.
caveats: ASC combines adhesives and sealants across North America rather than US toll adhesives alone.
The constant-quality service basket can shift as customers reformulate for VOC, PFAS, or sustainability requirements.
d5.change_evidence: ASC segment-level US volume forecasts combined with customer surveys of outsourcing share, target backlog, capacity utilization, and repeat-batch retention would materially move the range.
d5.rationale: Packaging, construction, transportation, electronics, infrastructure, and advanced manufacturing support growing adhesive quantity, while housing and durable-goods cycles, substitution, and customer insourcing create downside.

caveats: The provided firm count is an EBITDA-margin bridge, not an observed service-firm census.
Hybrid firms may have eligible toll operations alongside ineligible branded-product revenue.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level classification of the provided LMM population using business databases, websites, quality certificates, and revenue splits between proprietary products and external toll/private-label work would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Multiple US firms explicitly offer toll, contract, and private-label adhesive production, establishing a meaningful service subset. Most adhesive manufacturers nevertheless appear to commercialize their own formulations, and eligibility further requires repeat external revenue, LMM economics, independent ownership, and transferable quality and customer systems.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325520 ($2,228,064,659) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325520 ($19,309,246,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $640,078 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325520); class <5: 97 firms, avg revenue/firm $882,835 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $158,999 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,675,510 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $481,859 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 58 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,113,052 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,100,961 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 75 firms, avg revenue/firm $27,126,040 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,885,400 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $62,685,725 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,289,699 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $253,405,732 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $45,638,372 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 133

caveats: Some formulation screening, documentation, ordering, and technical support can shift to customer self-service.
Operator requirement does not imply unchanged staffing levels.
o.change_evidence: Widespread deployment of autonomous distributed adhesive microplants at customers, coupled with verified replacement of external toll volumes and remote self-service formulation, would materially lower this estimate.
o.rationale: The year-five service basket still requires permitted physical facilities, chemical handling, mixing, reaction or compounding, laboratory QC, filling, packaging, and accountable delivery. AI can reduce analysis and coordination labor but cannot provide adhesive production as software.

q.bridge: H.B. Fuller shows that raw materials make up about 75% of cost of sales and that adhesive revenue is sensitive to raw-material and end-market cycles; service providers show that customers may retain formula ownership in toll arrangements. I translate these product-company and service-model facts into retention after repricing: qualification, formulation know-how, and reliable small-batch execution support retention, but transparent toll economics, customer-owned formulas, procurement rebids, and material pass-through expose savings. H.B. Fuller's scale and proprietary portfolio likely overstate pricing power relative to LMM toll firms.
caveats: Contract structures differ between customer-owned formula tolling and manufacturer-owned private label.
The estimate excludes implementation and volume risk.
q.change_evidence: Contract and renewal data from at least five targets showing toll fees, open-book costing, material and energy pass-throughs, formula ownership, sole-source terms, rebates, and post-improvement gross margins would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Savings embedded in faster development, lower scrap, reliable QC, and responsive fulfillment are less visible and more retainable than direct labor reductions. In pure tolling, buyers can compare conversion charges and reclaim part of productivity gains at renewal.

rho.bridge: NIST's cross-manufacturing examples show achievable AI benefits but require defined problems, usable historic data, leadership, and incremental pilots. I apply those constraints to toll adhesive plants, with high implementation for office and retrieval tasks, moderate implementation for quality and formulation support, and low implementation for closed-loop process control. The general cases may overstate implementation where formulas are customer-confidential or production data are sparse.
caveats: Case studies are selected and do not establish a representative implementation rate.
This estimate excludes pricing and demand effects.
rho.change_evidence: Measured adoption and sustained hour savings from AI deployments at small specialty-chemical batch plants, including integration cost, model error, data completeness, operator use, and customer-approval requirements, would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Five years permits broad assistive deployment, but customer formula confidentiality, EHS controls, validation, limited IT staff, heterogeneous equipment, and the need to reproduce qualified products constrain automation of core process decisions.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell reports broad manufacturing transaction growth, while ASC records continuing adhesive-sector acquisitions. Neither measures the five-year transfer rate for US LMM toll manufacturers. I use this observed deal activity to support an active buyer universe, then discount for environmental diligence, customer qualification, founder dependence, and the possibility that transactions involve distributors or larger product companies. Broad SMB data may bias high for regulated plants, while disclosed sector news undercounts private deals.
caveats: The cited ASC acquisition is a distributor, demonstrating buyer appetite but not a manufacturing transfer rate.
No owner-age distribution specific to eligible adhesive firms was found.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year PitchBook and Capital IQ census of US adhesive-manufacturer control deals below $100 million enterprise value, paired with ownership age and a denominator of eligible firms, would materially move the probability.
s5.rationale: Specialty-chemical strategics and private equity value adhesive capabilities and customer relationships, and many independent firms are plausible succession targets. Yet liability, process know-how, and concentrated accounts can prevent or reshape a sale.

principal_driver: Growing, qualification-dependent adhesive demand preserves the need for physical contract plants while supporting recurring AI-assisted technical and quality workflows.
principal_weakness: Customer-owned formulas and transparent toll economics can make productivity savings easy to reprice away, while the eligible service subset is smaller than the full NAICS.
